subject: UX & Research
title: Search vs. Browse Behavior
description: A short lesson to help you apply Search vs. Browse Behavior.

hook.text: Some users always search. Others always browse. And most switch between the two depending on the task.

buildup.text: Known-item seekers search: 'iPhone 15 Pro Max case.' Explorers browse: 'What phone cases do you have?'

discovery.text: Research shows about 50% of users start with search on e-commerce sites, but 60% end up browsing categories too.

twist.text: Sites that hide categories and rely only on search lose explorers. Sites without search frustrate known-item seekers.

climax.text: Support both behaviors. Show search prominently and keep category navigation visible. Let users switch freely.

punchline.text: Some users search, some browse. Support both or lose half your visitors.

quiz.question: Why must sites support both search and browse behavior?
options: Different users and tasks require different finding strategies — some search, some browse
Search is legally required on all commercial websites
Browsing is always faster than searching
